metadata.career_relevance: The Files API from Topic 11.1 is built ON TOP of these lower-level streams. Network sockets, HTTP connections, console I/O, and streaming processing all use Reader/Writer/InputStream/OutputStream directly. When you read from a server response or write to a network connection, you're working with InputStream wrapped in a BufferedReader. After this topic, you understand the entire Java I/O stack — from raw bytes to convenient line-by-line reading.

hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll use BufferedReader for efficient line reading, BufferedWriter and PrintWriter for formatted output, InputStreamReader/OutputStreamWriter for byte-to-character conversion, understand why and how to buffer I/O, and read from any source — files, network, console — using the same API.

concept_explanation.io_hierarchy:
**The Java I/O hierarchy — two families:**

```
Byte streams (raw binary data):
├── InputStream  (read bytes)
│   ├── FileInputStream
│   ├── ByteArrayInputStream
│   └── SocketInputStream (network)
└── OutputStream (write bytes)
    ├── FileOutputStream
    ├── ByteArrayOutputStream
    └── SocketOutputStream

Character streams (text, with encoding):
├── Reader  (read chars — InputStream + encoding)
│   ├── FileReader
│   ├── InputStreamReader  ← converts bytes → chars
│   └── BufferedReader     ← adds buffering + readLine()
└── Writer  (write chars)
    ├── FileWriter
    ├── OutputStreamWriter ← converts chars → bytes
    ├── BufferedWriter     ← adds buffering + newLine()
    └── PrintWriter        ← adds print/println/printf
```

**The key bridge:**
- `InputStreamReader(InputStream)` → reads bytes, decodes as chars
- `OutputStreamWriter(OutputStream)` → encodes chars as bytes

The **decorator pattern**: each layer wraps the previous one, adding a capability. `BufferedReader` adds buffering. `InputStreamReader` adds encoding. They compose.

visual_aid.description: Show the decorator stack as nested boxes: innermost box = FileInputStream/SocketInputStream (raw bytes). Next layer = InputStreamReader (adds encoding, bytes→chars). Next layer = BufferedReader (adds 8KB buffer, readLine()). Arrow from left: 'raw bytes from disk/network' flows through each layer, emerging as 'String line' on the right. Parallel stack on output side: String content → PrintWriter → BufferedWriter → OutputStreamWriter → FileOutputStream/SocketOutputStream. Below: comparison table BufferedReader.readLine() vs Scanner.nextLine() — speed, use cases. Bottom: the three common starting points: FileInputStream (file), socket.getInputStream() (network), System.in (keyboard).
visual_aid.alt_text: I/O decorator stack from raw bytes to readable lines, output stack, and Scanner vs BufferedReader comparison

worked_example.scenario: Build a log analyser that reads a server log file line by line (efficient buffered reading), filters and summarises error entries, and writes a formatted report (PrintWriter). Demonstrates the full read-process-write I/O pipeline.
worked_example.problem_statement: Read an Apache-style server log, extract ERROR and WARN lines, count by level and endpoint, write a formatted summary report with PrintWriter.

step_by_step_solution.action: Generate sample log file and read with BufferedReader

worked_example.expected_output:
=== Log Analysis ===
  INFO    2
  ERROR   3
  WARN    1
--- Error endpoints ---
  /api/payment         2
  /api/products        1
Total lines: 6
Report: [N] bytes
things_to_notice: Files.newBufferedReader(path) is the preferred way to wrap a file in a BufferedReader — it handles encoding (UTF-8) automatically and integrates with the NIO Path API.
readLine() returns null at end of file — the while loop idiom `while ((line = br.readLine()) != null)` is the standard pattern for reading all lines.
PrintWriter wraps BufferedWriter wraps FileWriter — three layers. Each adds one capability: FileWriter opens the file, BufferedWriter adds buffering, PrintWriter adds println/printf.
PrintWriter.printf uses %n for newline (platform-specific), not \n. For files that will be read on the same OS, \n is fine; %n is more portable.

The entire analysis is a classic: read-aggregate-write. BufferedReader for efficient reading, stream operations for aggregation, PrintWriter for formatted output. This pattern appears in data processing everywhere.

industry_examples.use_case: When a Spring controller reads a request body: `request.getReader()` returns a BufferedReader wrapping the HTTP request InputStream. When writing a response: `response.getWriter()` returns a PrintWriter wrapping the HTTP response OutputStream. Every web request uses these exact classes — understanding them reveals how web frameworks work.

Seller CSV uploads arrive as HTTP multipart streams. The processing pipeline: InputStream → InputStreamReader → BufferedReader → readLine() loop → parse each product row. Writing responses back: PrintWriter wrapping ByteArrayOutputStream, convert to byte[] for response. The same stack as Topic 11.2, just with network streams instead of files.

industry_examples.company_context: Log aggregation and ELK stack integration
industry_examples.use_case: Log shippers (Filebeat, Logstash) read log files line by line. The Java equivalent: BufferedReader on a file, readLine() loop. Tailing a growing log file: check file size, seek to last position, BufferedReader from that offset. The Files.newBufferedReader pattern is exactly what monitoring agents use.

applications.interview_relevance:
I/O stream questions in interviews:

- **'Why wrap FileReader in a BufferedReader?'** — Performance: without buffering, each readLine() calls the OS for a few bytes. With BufferedReader, 8KB is read at once, served from the buffer. 100x faster for line-by-line reading.
- **'What is InputStreamReader?'** — Bridges bytes to characters. InputStream provides raw bytes; InputStreamReader decodes them using a charset (UTF-8 etc.) into chars. Essential when working with network streams.
- **'Difference between PrintWriter and BufferedWriter?'** — PrintWriter adds print/println/printf methods. BufferedWriter adds buffering (and newLine()). In production, wrap both: PrintWriter over BufferedWriter.
- **'What does readLine() return at end of file?'** → null. The while `((line = br.readLine()) != null)` pattern checks for this.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily BufferedReader/PrintWriter usage:

- Log file processing: `Files.newBufferedReader(logPath)` → readLine loop
- Console interaction: `new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in))`
- HTTP response reading: `new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(connection.getInputStream()))`
- Formatted output to file: `PrintWriter over BufferedWriter over FileWriter`
- Testing I/O code: `StringReader/StringWriter` instead of real files

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using FileReader or FileWriter directly (without BufferedReader/Writer) — 10-100x slower for large files.
common_gaps.remediation: Always wrap: `new BufferedReader(new FileReader(file))` or prefer `Files.newBufferedReader(path)`. FileReader/FileWriter alone are unbuffered — each read/write is a system call.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Slow performance reading large files; or using FileReader.read() char by char

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Calling scanner.nextLine() immediately after nextInt()/nextDouble() — gets an empty string because nextInt left the newline in the buffer.
common_gaps.remediation: After any nextInt()/nextDouble()/nextXxx(), call scanner.nextLine() to consume the leftover newline before reading the next line.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: nextLine() returns empty string unexpectedly after nextInt()

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Creating PrintWriter directly wrapping FileWriter — unbuffered and slow for large outputs.
common_gaps.remediation: Always: `new PrintWriter(new BufferedWriter(new FileWriter(file)))` — the BufferedWriter provides the buffer; PrintWriter provides the formatting methods.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Slow output when writing many lines

key_takeaways: InputStreamReader: bytes → chars (add encoding); OutputStreamWriter: chars → bytes
BufferedReader: wraps any Reader, adds 8KB buffer + readLine()
readLine() returns null at EOF — use while ((line = br.readLine()) != null)
BufferedWriter: wraps any Writer, adds buffer + newLine()
PrintWriter: wraps any Writer, adds print/println/printf
Scanner: convenient parsing but slower; use for small/interactive input
StringReader/StringWriter: in-memory I/O — great for testing
Always try-with-resources for any BufferedReader or Writer
Specify charset explicitly: StandardCharsets.UTF_8 (not system default)

self_check_questions.question: Why is `new BufferedReader(new FileReader('big.log'))` faster than `new FileReader('big.log')` alone?
self_check_questions.answer: FileReader reads from disk one character at a time — one system call per read. System calls are expensive (kernel context switches). BufferedReader reads 8KB at once into an in-memory buffer, then serves characters from that buffer. For a file with 100,000 lines, this means ~100,000 system calls with FileReader alone vs only ~100 with BufferedReader (8KB per call × 100 = 800KB). The speedup is typically 10-100x for sequential line reading.

It prints 'Got: ' (empty string). nextInt() reads the '42' but stops at the newline — the \n stays in the buffer. nextLine() immediately reads that leftover \n and returns an empty string. Fix: add `sc.nextLine()` after `sc.nextInt()` to consume the trailing newline before reading the actual next line.

self_check_questions.question: You receive an InputStream from a network socket and want to read line by line. What code do you write?

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: **CSV and Structured Data Processing** — CSV is the universal data exchange format. Reading it seems simple (split on comma) but real CSV has quoted fields, escaped commas, multi-line values, and varying encodings. Topic 11.3 covers proper CSV parsing with OpenCSV, reading real-world datasets, and writing output files. Plus a look at JSON — parsing with Jackson or Gson, the libraries you'll use for every REST API integration.
